title: Chapter 23: Subject 03
content: They were alone in his chamber—the smallest alcove in the Underlayer, barely room for two. Rain from the night above bled through a cracked vent, and the sound filled the silence between them like a conversation neither of them wanted to have.
Ash sat on the floor with his back against the wall. Wren sat across from him, knees drawn up, waiting.

She'd been waiting for days. Since the argument. Since the look on his face when he'd said *at least we know what we are.* She'd seen the door crack open, and she'd been patient—which was not something Wren had ever been good at.
Tonight, the door swung wide.
"My name was Alexander Chen," he said.
The words came out flat. Rehearsed, almost, like a statement read into a record.

"I was a data analyst. NeuroScale's predictive modeling division. Three years. I was good at it—pattern recognition, anomaly detection. The stuff they built their early algorithms on." His tattoos flickered, a weak pulse. "I found something I wasn't supposed to find."
Wren didn't speak. Didn't move. The rain kept falling through the vent.

"Anomalies in the clinical trial data. ANX-00. There were twelve subjects in the first phase. The official records showed four adverse events, all minor. Cognitive fog, temporary disorientation. The kind of thing you bury in an appendix and call manageable."
He dragged a hand down his face.

"The real data showed seven subjects who experienced total perceptual dissociation within seventy-two hours. They stopped being recognized by facial recognition. Biometric locks failed. Their own families walked past them in the street."
"Stage 3," Wren whispered.

"Full erasure in under a week. And nobody knew. Nobody could know, because the condition itself prevented the subjects from reporting it. You can't file a complaint when the system doesn't believe you exist."
Ash pulled his knees up, mirroring her posture. The chamber felt smaller.

"I documented everything. Copied the raw files, the internal memos, the email chains where the trial leads discussed 'subject attrition'—that's what they called it. Attrition. Like we were carpet wearing thin."
"We?"
"Them. The subjects. I wasn't one yet." A pause. "I planned to leak it. A journalist I trusted. A clean handoff. I was naive enough to think that would be enough."
"What happened?"
"I made the mistake of telling one person. Dr. Elaine Vance."
The name hit the air like a slap. Wren felt it land in her own body—a cold stab behind her ribs.
"Vance was in my division. Senior researcher. I thought she was an ally. She'd expressed concerns about the trial protocols in meetings. I thought—"
He stopped. Swallowed.
"She wasn't expressing concerns. She was fishing. Seeing who knew, how much, whether it could be contained."
The rain intensified. Water pooled near the vent and ran in a thin rivulet toward the drain in the corner.
"The night I was going to hand over the files, Vance invited me to a meeting. After hours. Building C, sublevel two. She said she had additional documentation that would strengthen the case."
Ash's voice had gone thin. A wire under tension.
"The room had a chair and an IV stand. Two orderlies. She told me it was a routine consultation. That she wanted to record my statement to strengthen the internal review."
He looked at his hands. His tattoos were dim—dimmer than she'd ever seen them.

"They strapped me down. Vance explained what was going to happen while they prepped the IV. She was very calm. Very clinical. She told me ANX-00 had shown promising results in subjects with high cognitive resilience—people whose minds fought the dissociation. Analysts. Pattern thinkers."
His breath shuddered.
"She said I was going to help them understand the mechanism better. A live data point. She said it would be easier if I didn't fight."
Wren's throat was raw. She tasted something acrid—adrenaline, maybe. Or just the sour residue of someone else's pain sitting in her own mouth.
"How much did they give you?"

"Four times the trial dose. They called it 'accelerated administration.'" A bitter laugh. "The normal progression—what happened to you, what happened to most invisibles—that takes weeks. Months. The slow fade. You feel yourself slipping, people look through you, then past you, then straight ahead like you were never there."
He held up his hand. Looked at it. The fingers trembled.
"They did it in one night. Injected me at 11:47 PM. I know because I was staring at the clock on the wall, trying to hold onto something. A number. Anything solid."
"What did it feel like?"
Ash lowered his hand.

"You know that moment when you walk into a room and forget why? That sensation of something being there—a thought, a purpose—and then it's gone, and you can't even remember the shape of what you lost? Imagine that happening to your entire self. Every relationship, every memory of being seen, every confirmation that you exist. Just... dissolving. Like salt in water."
"They kept me locked in that room for four days."
His voice was barely audible now. The rain almost drowned it.

"No food. No water the first two days—they wanted to observe the cognitive effects without variables. There was a camera in the corner and a microphone. Vance came in once a day to check my vitals and make notes. She talked into a recorder. 'Subject 03 continues to exhibit progressive perceptual dissociation. Recommend extended observation before release.'"
Subject 03. Not Alexander Chen. Not a person. A number.
"On the third day, I screamed for six hours and nobody came. On the fourth day, I stopped screaming. Something in me just... went quiet. Like a circuit breaker."
He pressed his fist against his sternum.
"When they let me out, I walked to the front desk of my apartment building. The doorman didn't see me. I stood in front of him for twenty minutes. He checked his phone. Yawned. Adjusted his tie."
Ash's gaze found hers across the dark chamber.

"I went to my mother's house. She was in the kitchen. I sat at the table where I'd eaten dinner every Sunday for twenty-six years. She made tea. Drank it alone. I said her name. She hummed along to the radio."
Wren couldn't breathe.

The rain. The cold. The way his voice never cracked—not once—even as he described the erasure of everything he'd ever been. That was the worst part. The composure. Like he'd rehearsed this so many times that the feelings had worn smooth.
"My family reported me missing three days later. I watched my mother give the interview from across the street. She was crying. She looked right through me."
He leaned his head back against the wall.

"My apartment was re-rented within a week. My bank accounts closed—no activity triggers automatic closure after seven days. My employee ID was deactivated. My health insurance terminated. My driver's license flagged as invalid. Within two weeks, I was legally dead. A certificate filed by one Dr. Elaine Vance, citing previously undiagnosed cardiac event. Body cremated per company health policy."
"You were right there," Wren said. "You were still alive."
"I'm still alive now." He met her gaze. "Aren't I?"
The silence that followed was enormous.

Wren felt it pressing against her chest, filling the chamber, pushing out the air. She thought about her own fading—the slow awful dissolve of the past months. But she'd had time. She'd had stages. She'd had the gradual horror of watching the world unsee her.
Ash had lost everything in a single night. While strapped to a chair. While a woman in a lab coat took notes.

"Vance watched," he said, as if reading her thoughts. "She sat in that room with her recorder and her clipboard and she watched me disappear. And when it was done, when I was nothing but data on her screen, she filed the paperwork that made it official."
His tattoos flared. Bright, angry blue-white.
"So you'll forgive me if I don't feel guilty about deleting a few bank accounts."
Wren moved without thinking.

She crossed the space between them and sat beside him—not across, beside. Shoulder to shoulder against the cold wall. She didn't touch him. Just sat there, close enough that she could feel the heat radiating off his skin, the faint electric hum of his tattoos.
Ash went rigid. Then, slowly, release. His shoulders dropped a fraction. His breathing steadied.
They sat like that for a long time, listening to the rain.
"I'm sorry," she said finally.
"Don't be. It's not your fault."
"It's not yours either."
He didn't answer. But his hand moved—just barely—until his knuckles rested against hers on the concrete floor.
Neither of them moved away.
The rain kept falling. Above them, the city hummed and glittered, oblivious to the dead man sitting in its basement.
To the woman who was learning how to mourn someone who wasn't gone yet.
